patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: constify oom_control, scan_control, and alloc_context nodemask

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: constify oom_control, scan_control, and alloc_context nodemask

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch makes the nodemasks in several Linux kernel structures const, ensuring they are not modified when passed around between contexts. The affected structures include oom_control, scan_control, and alloc_context. This change is motivated by the fact that these nodemasks can come from various sources, including tasks and cpusets, and should be treated as immutable. The patch modifies 11 files across several kernel directories to make this change.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: The patch introduces a new helper function, memory_block_aligned_range(), which aligns physical address ranges to memory block boundaries for hotplug operations. This is achieved by updating the start address up and end address down to the nearest memory block boundary. The helper is added to <linux/memory.h> and used in dax/kmem.c.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with Acked-by. The code looks good and aligns with existing memory hotplug operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block` function to accept an explicit online type through its argument, rather than relying on a default value. This change prepares for future scenarios where callers can specify custom online types. The patch also updates the `add_memory_resource` function to pass the default online type as a local variable. No functional changes are introduced by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function to allow drivers that use DAX and hotplug policies to build even when hotplug is disabled. The function returns `MMOP_OFFLINE` by default, which is non-destructive.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and added an Acked-by tag to indicate agreement with the change. The reviewer understood that the patch is intended for hotplug use cases without CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, __add_memory_driver_managed(), which allows drivers to select the preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The existing add_memory_driver_managed() function is refactored to use this new helper, and proper kernel documentation is added. This change is primarily for the CXL driver, but it's designed to be safe for in-tree modules that need to override the system-wide onlining policy.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add multi-range hotunplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to offline and remove multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation, ensuring atomicity and preventing race conditions that could leave some ranges in an inconsistent state. The new function, offline_and_remove_memory_ranges(), takes an array of ranges and processes them under a single lock, providing all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step. This is particularly useful for devices composed of multiple ranges, such as DAX or kmem, which can now call this function instead of looping over individual ranges. The patch addresses a problem where another daemon could online a block that was being offlined, causing the unplug operation to fail.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability for drivers using dax_kmem to specify an auto-online policy when creating DAX devices. It does this by adding an 'online_type' field to the dev_dax_data structure, which can be set to a specific memory hotplug online type. This allows drivers to choose their preferred auto-online policy instead of relying on the system default. The patch makes no functional changes to existing callers and is intended to provide more flexibility for DAX device creation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions in the dax/kmem driver by extracting initialization, cleanup, hotplug, and hot-remove logic into separate helper functions. The helpers enable future extensions to support more granular control over memory hotplug operations. The patch also addresses an issue where resources are not registered when probing occurs, preventing dax devices from being added to sysfs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to control and query the state of an entire region of memory managed by the dax kmem driver. The new 'hotplug' attribute allows userspace to set the state of the memory region, which can be either online (in one of three possible zones) or unplugged. The patch also introduces a new 'unplugged' state that only applies to kmem/hotplug and provides atomic hot-unplug functionality across the entire device. This addresses a race condition in the previous implementation where external entities could operate on memory blocks while they were being removed, leading to failed hot-unplug operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to verify the functionality of the dax/kmem hotplug sysfs attribute, which allows transitioning a kmem-backed dax device between 'unplugged', 'online' and 'online_movable' states. The test discovers an existing kmem-bound dax device, skips if none is found or memory cannot be freed, and then validates the interface contract by checking for correct behavior in various scenarios, including online -> unplug -> online_movable -> unplug cycle.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control via sysfs, allowing for transitions to be atomic across entire device ranges. The patch addresses a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which was discussed in the LPC2025 device memory sessions. It introduces a 'hotplug' attribute and updates mm and dax plumbing to support it. The state names mirror the per-block memoryX/state ABI with modifications for atomic whole-device control. The patch also includes mm preparation changes, such as adding helpers for online type and offline+remove of multiple ranges under a single lock.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 00/19] nfsd: more bugfixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/19] nfs/localio: fix nfsd_file ref leak on nfs_local_doio() init failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a reference leak in the NFS localio code by adding missing calls to nfs_local_file_put() and introducing a new cleanup label for error handling paths. The leak occurs when the function nfs_local_doio() returns early due to zero count or iocb initialization failure, causing the associated net namespace reference to be pinned and preventing network namespace teardown. The patch addresses these issues by properly releasing resources in all return paths, ensuring correct behavior and avoiding potential system crashes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/19] nfsd: clear opcnt on compound arg release to prevent OOB read
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where stale compound metadata could be exposed to userspace through the status interface. The issue occurs when an error path is taken after setting the RPC status counter to an odd value, causing an out-of-bounds read from adjacent slab memory. To prevent this, the patch clears the operation count field (opcnt) in the nfsd4_release_compoundargs function when releasing compound arguments.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/19] nfsd: add missing read barrier to rpc_status_get dumpit seqcount retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a missing read barrier to the rpc_status_get_dumpit function in nfsd, ensuring that all data reads complete before re-checking the status counter on weakly-ordered architectures. Without this barrier, concurrent modifications by the writer could cause the retry logic to be ineffective. The patch matches the barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/19] nfsd: fix netlink dumpit error handling for rpc_status_get
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an error handling issue in the NFS daemon's netlink dump functionality. When composing a message for rpc_status_get, the code would return -ENOBUFS on failure to add attributes without canceling the partial message, causing the netlink dump infrastructure to abort the entire dump. The patch corrects this by canceling the partial message and returning the number of bytes added if some messages were successfully added, allowing for pagination instead of a fatal error.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/19] sunrpc: defer rq_argp and rq_resp free until after RCU grace period
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the sunrpc code by deferring the freeing of rq_argp and rq_resp until after an RCU grace period, ensuring that any RCU readers have finished accessing these resources before they are freed. The patch introduces a new call_rcu callback to free these resources asynchronously, alongside the existing kfree_rcu for the svc_rqst struct.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/19] nfsd: check nfsd4_acl_to_attr() return value in nfsd4_create()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server code where ACL translation errors were being silently discarded during file creation. The patch adds an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr() to ensure that any errors are properly handled and do not cause the CREATE operation to proceed without the requested ACL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/19] nfsd: add filehandle match check to nfsd4_delegreturn()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a filehandle match check to the nfsd4_delegreturn() function in the NFSv4 state machine. The function previously did not verify that the delegation's file matches the current file handle, allowing a client to potentially destroy the correct delegation and wake up the wrong inode's waiters. The patch introduces an additional call to nfs4_check_fh() after generation check to ensure filehandle matching.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/19] nfsd: validate nseconds in TIME_DELEG decode paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds range checks to prevent a malformed timespec from being stored in the Linux kernel's NFS server (nfsd). The checks ensure that the 'nseconds' field, which represents nanoseconds, is less than NSEC_PER_SEC. This fix addresses a potential issue where a malformed timespec could propagate through notify_change() to disk. The patch modifies two files: nfs4callback.c and nfs4xdr.c, adding range checks in both SETATTR and CB_GETATTR paths. The changes were made to address an issue introduced by the 'nfsd: add support for delegated timestamps' commit (6ae30d6eb26b).

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/19] nfsd: remove premature NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED in CLAIM_PREVIOUS path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes a premature setting of the NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED flag in the CLAIM_PREVIOUS path of the nfsd kernel module. The flag is set too early, causing it to remain permanently confirmed even if an open operation fails. The correct success-path setter already exists in init_open_stateid(). This change only affects pre-NFSv4.1 sessions and fixes a bug introduced by a previous patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/19] nfsd: fix version mismatch loops in nfsd_acl_init_request()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_acl_init_request() function where the supported version range for PROG_MISMATCH is incorrectly calculated due to a version mismatch between rqstp->rq_vers and the loop variable i. The fix involves replacing rqstp->rq_vers with the loop variable i, matching the pattern used by the sibling nfsd_init_request() function. This patch addresses an issue introduced in commit e333f3bbefe3 ('nfsd: Allow containers to set supported nfs versions') and was assisted by Claude.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/19] nfsd: fix FL_SLEEP being set unconditionally for all LOCK types
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server code where the FL_SLEEP flag was being set unconditionally for all lock types, including non-blocking ones. The issue was caused by a bitmask test that was introduced in a previous commit. This patch replaces the bitmask test with explicit equality checks to ensure that the FL_SLEEP flag is only set when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/19] nfsd: add fh_want_write() for early-verified SETATTR in nfsd_proc_setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a call to fh_want_write() in the nfsd_proc_setattr() function, which is necessary because the early verification of SETATTR causes the mount write reference to be skipped. The missing call was introduced by a previous change that disabled an NFSv2 timestamp workaround for NFSv3+. This patch ensures that notify_change() runs with a valid mount write reference.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/19] nfsd: fix clock domain mismatch in clients_still_reclaiming()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where clients can hold the server in grace indefinitely by sending CLAIM_PREVIOUS OPEN requests, blocking all non-reclaim operations for other clients. The issue arises from a clock domain mismatch between CLOCK_REALTIME and CLOCK_BOOTTIME used in the deadline computation of the clients_still_reclaiming() function. To fix this, the patch adds a new boot_time_bt field to store the boot time in CLOCK_BOOTTIME and uses it alongside the existing boot_time for the deadline computation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 14/19] nfsd: use test_and_clear_bit for somebody_reclaimed to prevent lost update
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential issue in the NFS daemon where a concurrent set operation could be lost due to a race condition between testing and clearing a flag. The patch replaces separate test_bit() and clear_bit() calls with a single atomic test_and_clear_bit() call, ensuring that the read-and-clear operation is atomic and preventing lost updates.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 15/19] nfsd: reject reclaim LOCK after RECLAIM_COMPLETE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 server where it incorrectly accepts reclaim LOCK requests after RECLAIM_COMPLETE has been sent by a client. The fix adds a check to ensure that the per-client NFSD4_CLIENT_RECLAIM_COMPLETE bit is set before accepting a reclaim LOCK request, as required by RFC 8881. This prevents the server from accepting invalid reclaim requests and ensures data consistency.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 16/19] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon's netlink listener set functionality by validating the length of sockaddr structures per address family. The issue arose from an overly permissive policy that allowed a 16-byte NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR with sa_family=AF_INET6, causing an out-of-bounds read across multiple consumers. To fix this, the patch tightens the policy to require at least 16 bytes for NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR and adds per-family length validation in both nlmsg_for_each_attr_type loops.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 17/19] lockd, nfsd: RCU-protect nlmsvc_ops dispatch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free bug in the lockd and nfsd modules by RCU-protecting the nlmsvc_ops dispatch. The bug occurs when the nfsd module is removed, causing stale loads of the nlmsvc_ops pointer. To fix this, the patch declares nlmsvc_ops as __rcu, publishes it via rcu_assign_pointer(), and clears it via RCU_INIT_POINTER() + synchronize_rcu(). Additionally, a struct module *owner field is added to nlmsvc_binding and the module is pinned across indirect calls using try_module_get/module_put. This ensures that the binding is properly torn down when the module is removed, avoiding leaks of struct file references.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 18/19] nfsd: move nfsd_debugfs_init() after nfsd4_init_slabs() in init_nfsd()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the initialization of debugfs files in the NFS daemon to after slab allocation has succeeded, preventing orphaned debugfs files when slab allocation fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 19/19] nfsd: initialize DRC hash table before registering shrinker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug on weakly-ordered architectures where the DRC hash table is not fully initialized before it becomes visible to the shrinker, causing a NULL dereference. The fix involves moving the initialization of the bucket list heads and the drc_hashsize store before registering the shrinker. This ensures that the hash table is fully initialized before it can be accessed by the shrinker.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This is a pile of bugfixes found via LLM inspection. Most of these don't seem to be easily triggerable or require specific conditions, but they're still bugs nonetheless. Please consider these for v7.3.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Two early return paths in nfs_local_doio() fail to release the localio (nfsd_file) reference passed in by the caller: - When hdr->args.count is zero, the function returns 0 without calling nfs_local_file_put(). - When nfs_local_iocb_init() fails (e.g. -ENOMEM from allocation or -EOPNOTSUPP if the file lacks read_iter/write_iter), the function returns the error without releasing localio or completing the hdr lifecycle. A leaked nfsd_file pins the associated net namespace reference, blocking network namespace teardown, and holds a reference on the exported filesystem, preventing unmount. Fix the zero-count path by adding the missing nfs_local_file_put() call.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8] array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+ compounds). If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8]
array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves
args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+
compounds).

If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when
nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC
status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
Since iops only has 8 elements and is the last field in struct
nfsd4_compoundargs, reading indices 8-15 accesses adjacent slab memory
and leaks it to userspace via netlink.

Zero opcnt unconditionally in nfsd4_release_compoundargs() so stale
compound metadata is never exposed through the status interface.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit() is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb() to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.
review_comments.raw_body:
The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()
is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter
check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb()
to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.

Without this barrier, on weakly-ordered architectures (ARM, POWER),
the CPU may reorder field reads past the second counter check, making
the retry logic ineffective: it could observe a consistent counter pair
while reading fields that have been concurrently modified by the writer.

Add smp_rmb() before the second smp_load_acquire() to match the
barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.

Fixes: ac18892ea3f7 ("NFSD: add rpc_status netlink support")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

 fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c | 6 ++++--
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
index c06d25c06f06..a4b5b1467fe2 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
@@ -1576,9 +1576,11 @@ int nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
 #endif /* CONFIG_NFSD_V4 */
 
 			/*
-			 * Acquire rq_status_counter before reporting the rqst
-			 * fields to the user.
+			 * Ensure all field reads complete before re-checking
+			 * the status counter. Pairs with the smp_store_release
+			 * in nfsd_dispatch to form a seq-lock like protocol.
 			 */
+			smp_rmb();
 			if (smp_load_acquire(&rqstp->rq_status_counter) !=
 			    status_counter)
 				continue;

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd_genl_rpc_status_compose_msg() returns -ENOBUFS on nla_put failure without calling genlmsg_cancel(), leaving a partial message in the skb. The caller then propagates -ENOBUFS directly, which the netlink dump infrastructure treats as a fatal error, aborting the entire dump. The correct netlink dump convention is: - Cancel any partial message with genlmsg_cancel() - If prior messages were added to the skb (skb->len > 0), save the current iterator position and return skb->len to paginate - Only return a negative errno when no messages fit at all Fix compose_msg to cancel the partial message on all nla_put failure paths, and fix the caller to paginate when possible rather than returning a fatal error. Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8 --- fs/nfsd/nfsctl.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: svc_rqst_free() frees rqstp->rq_argp and rqstp->rq_resp synchronously via kfree(), but defers the rqstp struct free via kfree_rcu().  After svc_exit_thread() calls list_del_rcu() and svc_rqst_free(), there is a window where RCU readers that started before list_del_rcu() can still traverse the thread list and find the rqstp.  These readers (e.g. nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()) dereference rqstp->rq_argp, which has already been freed \u2014 a use-after-free. Fix this by moving the kfree of rq_argp and rq_resp into an explicit call_rcu() callback alongside the struct free.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd4_create() stores the return value of nfsd4_acl_to_attr() in status, but the switch(create->cr_type) block unconditionally overwrites it in every branch. ACL translation errors are silently discarded, and the CREATE proceeds without the requested ACL.

review_comments.summary: nfsd4_delegreturn() is the only stateful NFSv4 operation that does not call nfs4_check_fh() to verify the delegation's file matches cstate->current_fh. A client can DELEGRETURN with a mismatched filehandle, destroying the correct delegation but waking the wrong inode's waiters.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_delegreturn() is the only stateful NFSv4 operation that does
not call nfs4_check_fh() to verify the delegation's file matches

inode's waiters.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) and per-procedure call counts to the sunrpc service statistics. It introduces a new pointer array in struct svc_stat to store these counts, which are managed by two helper functions: svc_stat_alloc_counts() and svc_stat_free_counts(). The patch also updates the nfsd_net_init() and nfsd_net_exit() functions to allocate and free these counts as needed. This change is necessary for an upcoming netlink stats interface that requires namespace-scoped statistics.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace arrays instead of global ones. This change allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts on a per-network-namespace basis, which is useful for monitoring and debugging purposes. The patch only affects the nfsd service, as it always allocates the necessary data structures in its initialization code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements a netlink handler to expose NFS server statistics through the nfsd generic netlink family. The handler uses a dump operation to stream statistics across multiple netlink messages, allowing for efficient retrieval of server statistics. The patch also includes documentation and updates to various files to support this new functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters in various NFS-related modules, as it is no longer accessed by any code. The removal of this field simplifies the code and reduces memory usage.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink instead of /proc/net/rpc/nfsd. The new interface provides per-network-namespace procedure call counts and exposes them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family. This allows nfsstat to retrieve server statistics via netlink with a procfs fallback for older kernels. The patches add per-netns counters, switch svc_seq_show() to read from these counters, implement a server-stats-get netlink dump handler, and remove unused global arrays.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: remove request-less entries from ent_w_req_queue to fix NULL deref

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a null pointer dereference issue in the FUSE implementation by removing request-less entries from the ent_w_req_queue list when a copy operation fails, and changing their state to invalid before terminating the request.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the io-uring implementation for FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) where a fuse request is not properly ended when an io-uring task is cancelled. When an io-uring task is cancelled, the fuse request remains linked to the processing queue and its reference count is never decremented, leading to resource leaks and potential crashes. The patch fixes this by releasing the entry directly from the cancel branch, removing it from the queue, completing the io-uring command, ending the fuse request, freeing the entry, and dropping its queue references.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a long-standing issue in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup during a race condition between list_lru_add() and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the 'effective' memcg from the locking function and having the caller use it to set the shrinker bit.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by sharing code between MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch flattens the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop by removing a top-level if statement and using an existing label to simplify the code structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

From: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>
Date: Tue, 9 Jun 2026 10:56:45 +0800
Subject: [PATCH] mm: prepare deferred split metadata only for anon swapin

__swap_cache_alloc() prepares deferred split metadata for large swapcache
folios.

That also covers shmem swapin, because shmem_swap_alloc_folio() can call
swapin_sync() with a large order[1]. But shmem folios are not queued on
the deferred split queue, so preparing the metadata doesn't buy us
anything there.

So let's limit it to anon swapin.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260601132135.14272-1-lance.yang@linux.dev/

Signed-off-by: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>
---
 mm/swap_state.c | 3 ++-
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
index 9c3a5cf99778..7adac957c2b8 100644
--- a/mm/swap_state.c
+++ b/mm/swap_state.c
@@ -465,7 +465,8 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_alloc(struct swap_cluster_info *ci,
 		return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);
 	}
 
-	if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
+	if (order > 1 && vma && vma_is_anonymous(vma) &&
+	    folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
 		spin_lock(&ci->lock);
 		__swap_cache_do_del_folio(ci, folio, entry, shadow);
 		spin_unlock(&ci->lock);
--

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the

> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:16PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
>queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
>on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
>can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
>
>        alloc/unmap
>          deferred_split_folio()
>            list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>            set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>
>        for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>          mem_cgroup_iter()
>            shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>              shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                  deferred_split_scan()
>                    walks memcg->split_queue
>
>The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
>has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
>by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
>
>list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
>streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
>widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
>queue as well.
>
>The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
>object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
>folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
>created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
>
>These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
>the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
>

>Tested-by: Mikhail Zaslonko <zaslonko@linux.ibm.com>

review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:07PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>This is version 5 of switching the THP shrinker to list_lru.
>
>Core of the new version is the list_lru/set_shrinker_bit fix up front,
>which minimally affects later patches; and a rebase onto the latest
>mm-unstable - replaced alloc_swap_folio() with __swap_cache_alloc().
>
>The changes seemed small enough that *I chose to keep the review tags
>from v4*. Please shout if you object to this!
>
>Changes in v5:
>- patch 1 is a new fix for a very old, pre-existing set_shrinker_bit()
>  problem in list_lru, where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg
>  instead of the ancestor that actually received the item. Pointed out
>  by Usama Arif and Sashiko; fix it first to make it minimally
>  backportable and so the conversion is safe.
>- patches 6 and 9 adapt to that fix's new memcg-by-reference
>  lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature
>- collapse_huge_page(): propagate folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure
>  as SCAN_ALLOC_HUGE_PAGE_FAIL instead of leaking SCAN_SUCCEED and
>  falsely reporting a successful MADV_COLLAPSE (Usama Arif, Sashiko)
>- deferred_split_isolate(): fix a UAF by reading folio state before
>  list_lru_isolate(); once removed, a racing folio_put() frees the
>  folio via the lockless list_empty() check while we still touch its
>  flags and stats (Sashiko)
>- rebased to mm-unstable of 2026-05-27, which simplifies the flatten
>  prep patch (now anon-only, as alloc_swap_folio() was folded into the
>  new __swap_cache_alloc()) and moves the swap-side
>  folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() hook into __swap_cache_alloc(). Kairui,
>  I would appreciate an eyeball on that.
>
>Changes in v4:
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with mem_cgroup_disabled() to fix
>  NULL deref in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() when booting with
>  cgroup_disable=memory (e.g., kdump capture kernel) -- reported and
>  tested by Mikhail Zaslonko on s390 and x86
>- flatten if (folio) branches in alloc_swap_folio() and alloc_anon_folio()
>  in a prep patch so the list_lru allocation additions are a clean minimal
>  diff (Lorenzo)
>- folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() moved out of alloc_charge_folio() into the
>  anon-only collapse_huge_page() path; collapse_file() shares that helper
>  but its pages don't go on the THP shrinker queue (David)
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with order > 1; mTHPs below order-2
>  can't be queued on the deferred split list (David)
>- make deferred_split_lru static, hide behind folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()
>  wrapper with GFP_KERNEL (Lorenzo)
>- rename l -> lru throughout huge_memory.c (Lorenzo)
>- kdoc for folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (Lorenzo)
>- list_lru_lock_irq()/unlock_irq()/add_irq() irq-disabling variants;
>  use list_lru_add_irq() in deferred_split_scan() (Lorenzo)
>- reorder shrinker_free() before list_lru_destroy() (Lorenzo)
>
>Changes in v3:
>- dedicated lockdep_key for irqsafe deferred_split_lru.lock (syzbot)
>- conditional list_lru ops in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (syzbot)
>- annotate runs of inscrutable false, NULL, false function arguments (David)
>- rename to folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (David)
>
>Changes in v2:
>- explicit rcu_read_lock() in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (Usama)
>- split out list_lru prep bits (Dave)
>
>The open-coded deferred split queue has issues. It's not NUMA-aware
>(when cgroup is enabled), and it's more complicated in the callsites
>interacting with it. Switching to list_lru fixes the NUMA problem and
>streamlines things. It also simplifies planned shrinker work.
>
>Patch 1 fixes a pre-existing list_lru bug where the shrinker bit is
>set on the caller's memcg rather than the ancestor whose sublist the
>item actually lands on after a walk-up. Standalone, backportable; the
>rest of the series depends on it.
>
>Patches 2-5 are cleanups and small refactors in list_lru code. They're
>basically independent, but make the THP shrinker conversion easier.
>
>Patch 6 extends the list_lru API to allow the caller to control the
>locking scope. The THP shrinker has private state it needs to keep
>synchronized with the LRU state.
>
>Patch 7 extends the list_lru API with a convenience helper to do
>list_lru head allocation (memcg_list_lru_alloc) when coming from a
>folio. Anon THPs are instantiated in several places, and with the
>folio reparenting patches pending, folio_memcg() access is now a more
>delicate dance. This avoids having to replicate that dance everywhere.
>
>Patch 8 flattens the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop so the next patch's
>list_lru hook lands as a clean addition rather than nested deep inside
>an if (folio) block.
>
>Patch 9 finally switches the deferred_split_queue to list_lru.

As the changelog above says, the old queue is per-memcg only, rather
than per-memcg-per-node. So reclaim on one node can still walk the whole
memcg queue and split underused THPs from other nodes in the same memcg.

But I think the new one can lose reclaim in the cgroup.memory=nokmem
case ...

With nokmem, the deferred shrinker can still run from memcg reclaim,
because it is SHRINKER_NONSLAB. But the list_lru is no longer per-memcg:

__list_lru_init() clears memcg_aware,

	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
		memcg_aware = false;

so list_lru_from_memcg_idx() falls back to the shared node list:

static inline struct list_lru_one *
list_lru_from_memcg_idx(struct list_lru *lru, int nid, int idx)
{
	if (list_lru_memcg_aware(lru) && idx >= 0) {
[...]
	}
	return &lru->node[nid].lru;
}

That makes the shrinker bit unreliable. __list_lru_add() still sets the
bit on the memcg passed in, but only when the list goes from empty to
non-empty:

bool __list_lru_add(struct list_lru *lru, struct list_lru_one *l,
		    struct list_head *item, int nid,
		    struct mem_cgroup *memcg)
{
	if (list_empty(item)) {
[...]
		if (!l->nr_items++)
			set_shrinker_bit(memcg, nid, lru_shrinker_id(lru));
[...]
		return true;
	}
	return false;
}

If memcg A adds the first folio, A gets the bit. If memcg B later adds a
folio to the same shared list, B does not get a bit, because the list
was already non-empty.

So in the A-first/B-later case, reclaim from B may not call the deferred
shrinker at all. The shared list is scanned from memcg reclaim only if
reclaim runs from the memcg that has the bit, such as A here, or from
global reclaim :)

Anyway, only after the shared list is emptied does the next memcg to add
a folio get to be the one with the bit, IIUC :)

Hopefully I didn't miss somthing important ...

Cheers, Lance

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_drain_all_queue. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. The second tracepoint captures when per-CPU drain work is queued for remote drain operations, including the requester stack and target CPU. These additions improve debugging capabilities by providing more detailed information about memory management events.

review_comments.author: Barry Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Barry Song questioned whether tracing each CPU individually is necessary, suggesting that tracing the entire __lru_add_drain_all() invocation would be sufficient, and also asked about tracing this_gen and lru_drain_gen.
review_comments.raw_body:
Do you need tracing on each CPU individually, or is tracing the
entire __lru_add_drain_all() invocation sufficient?

Do you also need this_gen and lru_drain_gen to be traced?

By the way, I'm not sure drain_all_queue is the best name here.
Why not simply use add_drain_all()? It would match the existing
function name better.

Best Regards
Barry

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agrees that using the mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint to show kworker stacks is sufficient and no further changes are needed.

Author acknowledged that trace parameters for mm_lru_drain_all_queue do not provide meaningful information about who is making requests to drain all, but plans to move the trace call earlier in the function to still capture requestors.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the latter would be fine. The remote work will invoke the
mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint, which will show up as kworker stacks. Since
the event already has the CPU, we could see where queued drains actually
ran.

---

As trace parameters, I don't think they give meaningful info on who is
making requests to drain all. But since I'm going to move the trace call
from within the CPU loop to earlier in the function we can still see
requestors even if the function exits early because a parallel drain
generation already satisfied the request.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author considered the feedback that a tracepoint may not be needed for the mm_lru_drain_all_queue event, as other probes can be installed on function invocation and read the single argument; agreed to drop this from v2 and keep the single mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually if it's just a single invocation and the only event data is the
force flag, a tracepoint may not even be needed. Other probes can be
installed on function invocation and read the single argument. I can
drop this from v2 and keep the single mm_lru_add_drain tracepoint.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that tracing only lru_add_drain_cpu() would not provide information on the requester of the drain, and suggested instead tracing the whole __lru_add_drain_all() function to capture this context.
review_comments.raw_body:
No we do want to trace the callers requesting to drain from all the CPUs. If you
trace just lru_add_drain_cpu() then you will only see that the drain is
requested for a given CPU but no information on the requester. 

Also as Barry said, I think single trace for whole __lru_add_drain_all() is good
enough.
